text: The surgeon injects vasopressin into the plane just above the capsule of a nine-centimeter intramural myoma located on the fundal and anterior wall of the uterus during a laparoscopic myomectomy.

The surgeon makes an oblique incision on the uterus using the vibrating jaw of the harmonic scalpel, causing the uterus to turn marble white. A crescent-shaped incision is visible, and fluid leakage is observed from the vasopressin introduced earlier.

The surgeon prepares a saline solution with two ampoules and applies the myoma screw to elevate the uterus. A circumferential incision is made on the anterior wall of the uterus, with the left hand supporting the myoma screw and the right hand making the incision. The incisions converge, leaving a portion of the serosa attached to the uterus.

The surgeon lifts the uterus with anterior traction using the left hand while employing harmonic suction irrigation to slowly separate the myoma from the uterine tissue. The myoma screw is repeatedly adjusted and applied at the base to optimize traction during the separation process.

The surgeon carefully separates the remaining portion of the myoma from the uterine base using a harmonic device, avoiding abrupt disruption of the tissue plane to prevent stripping of the endometrium and minimize bleeding from feeding vessels.

The surgeon repositions the myoma screw and uses the harmonic device to separate the remaining attachment of the myoma from the capsule. The sigmoid colon is visible below, requiring careful anterior traction to prevent injury during the separation process.

The surgeon separates the biomass tube and begins suturing using a double layer intracorporeal continuous technique with a two-third circle needle. A number one Vicryl suture with a 30 mm needle is employed for the closure.

The surgeon performs continuous suturing of the muscle layer of the uterus, ensuring each suture is tightened and properly approximated. The internal layer is sutured without opening the cavity, followed by the serous layer.

The surgeon exits the needle through the serous layer after the muscle layer is exposed, continuing suturing by pulling the suture with the open jaw of the Maryland forceps to tighten it. The suture is held to maintain traction while passing the needle, using a three-port technique without a fourth port.
Extracorporeal knot is also good for laparoscopic myomectomy. But you can use the intracorporeal as well with the continuous suture.

The surgeon performs double layer continuous suturing using intracorporeal technique, holding the suture with the right hand and tightening it with the open jaw of the Maryland or needle holder. The tissue is pressed and the suture is pulled out to ensure tightness, completing the suturing process.

The surgeon cuts and removes the suture, ensuring careful handling to avoid pricking the iliac vessel. Suction irrigation is performed to remove blood clots, with copious lavage repeated two to three times to clear the surgical field. Extra tissue visible as bulging is expected to dissolve soon.

The surgeon begins the process of morselation on a nine-centimeter myoma, using a laparoscopic morselator. The morselation is shown in progress, with the myoma being gradually reduced in size. The procedure requires a 15 mm port, which will be closed at the end to prevent hernia formation.

So this is a case of laparoscopic myomectomy in which a nine centimeter intramural myoma was removed and the closure of the wound was performed with the intracorporeal suturing. In this patient, there was no blood transfusion performed. Patient was in the leaf pairs post-operatively. Next day after surgery only, patient was discharged. So thank you very much for watching this video. We are not showing you entire morselation. And here it is the last part. Just the suction irrigation to have some collected blood out. And then no drain is used. And the next day, patient is discharged. So thank you very much for watching a video. This is a simple case of laparoscopic myomectomy. Have a nice day.
